nodes.body: Vera Lindqvist swings her case onto the last carriage of the last northbound train of the season. The whistle that sends it off sounds thin against a sky that cannot decide between sleet and snow. Behind her on the platform, the station clock has stopped at an hour she does not recognize, its hands fixed as if caught mid-breath. No one else seems to notice or to mind. The train pulls out anyway, past a signboard she has no time to read. The dark outside thickens with every mile, thickens and then something in it changes. Inside, the carriages are strangely empty for a night like this one, a scattering of passengers in cars built for hundreds, each keeping to some private errand the cold has not managed to freeze out of them. Vera settles into her seat and takes the measure of the compartment the way a person takes the measure of changing weather, sensing before she can name it that this ordinary journey is not going to stay ordinary. Down the corridor, a conductor she has not yet met is calling an hour that does not match her watch. Past the curtains of the sleeper cars, made-up berths wait in a silence that feels deliberate, arranged. On the seat across from her, someone has left a letter, folded once, addressed to no one she can name. And past the glass, the whole black country is sliding by, changing its shape mile on mile, as though the season itself cannot decide what it wants to become tonight.

Vera turns down the corridor toward the conductor's voice. The whole carriage leans with the curve of the track as if urging her on. The window beside her holds her own reflection over the black nothing outside, doubled and pale in the cold glass, and for a moment she watches herself watching him instead of the country beyond. He is checking his silver punch-clock against the light of a lamp that gutters when the train sways, snapping the case shut, opening it again, as though the hour it shows will change if he looks hard enough. It is an ordinary gesture, the kind any conductor might make a hundred times a shift, except that his mouth moves faintly with the numbers. The numbers he mouths are not the ones stamped into any timetable Vera has ever read. She finds she cannot look away from the small silver case in his hand.

There are two ways to press at the conductor and his stubborn clock. Vera weighs them the way she weighs most things, quickly, and then more slowly than she meant to. She could simply ask him outright why the numbers on his silver case do not match the hour on her own watch, and see what a direct question does to a man who seems, on the whole, to prefer being left alone with his instrument. Or she could hold back, let him finish his round without her interference, and trust that watching a thing long enough will eventually make it explain itself, the way frost on a window will eventually resolve into a shape if you do not blink. Pressing in risks his notice, the flicker of a man who does not want to be caught keeping strange time. Hanging back risks nothing but the slow erosion of the hour, and the chance that whatever she might have learned will pass her by while she waits for it to arrive on its own. The train sways on, indifferent to which kind of courage she chooses.

Vera asks him plainly. The conductor's hand stills over the punch-clock's case before he answers, which tells her almost as much as the answer does. The clock, he says, was stopped by the ninth stationmaster north of here, and has not been restarted since, not because it is broken but because restarting it was never anyone's job to finish. He says this the way a man recites a fact he has repeated so often it has worn smooth, then snaps the case shut and moves on down the carriage without waiting to see what she makes of it. The fact will not sit still in her: a clock, deliberately left unwound, kept by a man paid to keep it. It points her further into the train, past this one small wrongness, toward whatever waits at the place the ninth stationmaster left unfinished.

From a few seats back, Vera watches the conductor work his way down the aisle, snapping the silver case open and shut, open and shut, never quite satisfied with what it tells him. She has learned something just by watching: his ritual with the clock is not performance for passengers but a private compulsion, the working of a man checking a wound that will not close. She could carry that observation forward, fold it into the strange arithmetic of the night, and see where it leads next. Or she could let the lateness of the hour do what lateness always does, let the rhythm of wheel on rail smooth the edges of what she has noticed until it no longer troubles her, and drift instead toward the easy, dangerous comfort of sleep. The carriage is warm in the way overheated trains are warm. Her eyelids have grown heavier with every mile since the platform clock stopped.

choices.label: Hold the thread and go on.

nodes.body: However Vera has come to it, by asking outright or by watching from the shadow of her own seat, what she now knows about the conductor and his silver punch-clock settles into the same small, specific wrongness: a clock kept by a man whose job is keeping it, and kept stopped on purpose. It is not the kind of fact that explains a night like this one. It is the kind that will not be argued back out of her, the kind that changes the weight of every ordinary thing around it. The lamp gutters again as the train takes a curve, and for a moment the whole carriage seems to hold itself very still, the way a person holds a breath before saying something they cannot take back. Vera holds it too. Something in her decides, without quite asking permission, to go on.

She carries the fact of the stopped clock with her down the length of the train, through one swaying vestibule and then another, past doors that stick with cold and doors that do not stick at all. The dining car is dark except for a single working lamp. The sleeper cars breathe their curtained silence. Somewhere ahead, she can just make out the sound of a child's voice, low and unbothered, threading under the wheels' rhythm. None of it explains the ninth stationmaster, or why a train this empty needs a conductor at all. Each detail seems to lean the same direction, north and further north, toward whatever the night has actually been arranging since the platform clock first refused to move. Vera steadies herself against a handrail as the whole carriage shudders round a long curve, and understands, with the plain certainty of someone who has stopped pretending otherwise, that she is being asked a larger question than the one she started with.

The warmth of the carriage wins in the end. Vera means only to close her eyes for a moment, to rest them against the ache of watching the silver case open and shut, open and shut. The moment lengthens and folds over her before she can stop it. When she wakes, the light through the window has changed to a flat, ordinary grey. The conductor is nowhere in the carriage, nor is the strange attention she had been holding onto so carefully only minutes before. She checks her watch out of habit. It agrees with nothing, and troubles her less than it should. Whatever she had almost understood about the stopped clock has gone the way sleep takes everything, quietly, without asking. She arrives at wherever this is unharmed, entirely safe, and empty in a way she cannot quite name.

Vera walks toward the long row of curtained sleeper berths. The carriage sways beneath her with each joint in the rail, a rhythm she is already starting to trust more than she should. In the window at the end of the corridor her own reflection rides along over the dark, pale and doubled against a landscape she cannot make out. She catches herself studying that ghost of herself instead of the berths ahead. Every curtain along the row is drawn, neat as a row of closed eyes, save for one, three berths down, hanging slightly open on its rail. Nothing shows through the gap but shadow and the corner of a folded blanket. Something about that single unclosed curtain among all the closed ones keeps drawing her eye back to it, the way a single wrong note keeps drawing an ear back to a piece of music otherwise correctly played.

There are two ways to read that one open curtain. Vera can feel herself choosing between them even before she names the choice. She could draw it back herself, quickly, and see plainly what the berth holds before anyone can tell her not to look. That is the direct way. It risks whatever attention a stranger's hand on a stranger's curtain might draw in a carriage this quiet. Or she could pass it by for now, noting only what a passing glance allows, and trust that a berth left half open on a night like this one will show itself fully in its own time, the way things left unattended on a train usually do. The patient way costs her nothing but the ache of not knowing yet, and lets whatever is inside that berth remain the train's secret a little longer, rather than becoming hers before she is ready to hold it. Either way, the curtain does not move on its own to help her decide.

Vera draws the curtain back herself. What she finds is almost worse for being so ordinary: a berth made up and slept in, blanket folded down with a traveler's habitual care, and beside the pillow a single boarding ticket, punched through for a station three stops past the end of the published line. No coat, no case, no sign of whoever last lay here, only that ticket and the clean, cold hollow their body left in the mattress. She turns the ticket over in her fingers, half expecting the station's name to rearrange itself into something she recognizes. It holds its shape stubbornly, printed as plainly as any name on any ordinary line. It is a hard fact and a small one, a station that should not exist stamped plainly into paper. It will not sit still in her. It points forward, past this berth, toward whatever keeps a line running past its own last stop.

Vera leaves the curtain as it is and watches instead, from a little distance, the shape the light makes moving across that half-open gap each time the train takes a curve. She learns something even without touching the fabric: whoever last slept there left in a hurry, or did not mean to leave the curtain open at all. That thought joins itself to the rest of what she has begun to notice about this train. The blanket's folded corner shifts a fraction with every sway, as if the berth itself were still faintly occupied by the idea of someone. She could carry that small observation forward and let it sharpen whatever comes next. Or she could give in to the hour, let the sway of the carriage and the low murmur of the wheels do what they have clearly been doing to every other half-attentive passenger tonight, and drift off before the thought has finished forming. The corridor is warm and dim and entirely willing to let her stop thinking, if she lets it.

choices.label: Hold the thread and go on.

nodes.body: Whether she pulled the curtain back herself or only watched the gap it left, what Vera now understands about that berth settles into the same uneasy shape: someone slept here recently, and left for a station that has no business existing on any line she has ever traveled. It is a small wrongness, a single loose thread in an otherwise ordinary night. It will not stay small once she has noticed it, the way one crooked picture on a wall makes the whole room feel tilted, so that every other berth along the row now seems to be waiting on the same unanswered question. The train takes another long curve and the row of curtains sways together, in and out, like something breathing in its sleep. Vera feels the journey close gently around the thought, the way a held breath closes around itself. She understands, without being told, that she is not finished with this yet.

She lets the curtain fall back into its place, more or less as she found it. She carries the thought of that impossible ticket with her down the corridor, through one vestibule and then another, the couplings groaning under the cold. Ahead, she can hear the low clink of dishes in an unlit dining car, and further still the small, unbothered voice of a child who does not sound as though she should be awake, let alone alone. None of it tells her outright what the berth's missing passenger has to do with a stopped clock or a season that cannot decide what it is becoming. Each detail seems to point the same way, further north, further into the night, as if the whole train were quietly arranging itself around a single unasked question. Vera steadies herself against the cold brass rail as the carriage sways, and goes to meet whatever that question turns out to be.

The warmth of the corridor and the long, even sway of the carriage do what they have clearly been doing to every drowsy passenger tonight, and Vera's eyes close before she means them to. She dreams, briefly and without detail, of a door standing open somewhere just out of reach, and then there is no door at all, only the flat grey light of a morning she did not choose to arrive at. When she wakes, the curtain three berths down is drawn shut like all the others. No ticket, no hollow in the mattress, nothing remains to prove she ever saw what she thought she saw. She is unharmed, and warm, and entirely safe. She carries off the train only the faint, useless certainty that somewhere behind her a door stood open once, and that she is the one who let it close.

ending.title: A Door You Did Not Open

nodes.body: Vera reaches instead for the letter someone has left folded on the seat across from her. The carriage sways under the motion of picking it up, as though the train itself were curious to see what she does next. In the black window beside her, her own reflection leans in too, pale and doubled over the dark rushing past, watching her hands the way she is watching them herself. The paper is thin and much folded, softened at the creases the way paper gets when it has been carried a long time and read more than once, though the ink across its face still looks fresh, almost wet. There is no name on the outside, no address, nothing to say who left it or for whom, only a single line showing through where the fold does not quite meet: something about a north line, and a promise. That half-hidden line is what will not let her put the letter down again.

There are two ways to read a letter that is not addressed to you. Vera feels the pull of both at once. She could unfold it fully, right here, and take in whatever it says in one direct pass, the way a person tears a bandage free rather than easing it. That is the quicker way, and the riskier one, since a letter meant for someone else has a way of making the reader feel caught, even alone in an empty carriage. Or she could turn it over slowly first, study the softened creases and the half-showing line before committing to the whole of it, and let the letter's meaning arrive at its own unhurried pace, the way patient readers let a difficult book. Reading it outright might tell her everything at once, and risk whatever notice a stranger's private words draw when handled too quickly. Waiting costs her only time. The small, persistent ache of a sentence she has seen half of and cannot yet finish.

Vera unfolds the letter the rest of the way and reads it straight through. One line refuses to loosen its grip on her afterward: a promise, made to somebody unnamed, that the writer would be on the last train north whatever the cost of the ticket, and would not get off again until the promise was kept. There is no date, no signature, nothing to say whether that promise was kept or broken, only the flat certainty of ink laid down by someone who meant every word of it. It is a small fact and a hard one, this idea of a promise heavy enough to survive its own writer. It will not sit still in her mind. It points her further into the train, toward whoever might still be waiting to see it honored.

Vera turns the letter over instead of unfolding it fully, studying the shape of the half-hidden line without yet committing to the rest of the page. She learns something even from that much: the hand which wrote it was steady, careful, unhurried, the hand of someone who believed absolutely in what they were writing down. Even the crease itself seems deliberate, folded to hide exactly this much and no more, as though whoever wrote it wanted the promise glimpsed before it was ever fully read. She could carry that impression forward, fold it into everything else the night has shown her, and let it deepen on its own. Or she could let the letter rest in her lap unfinished. She could let the warmth of the carriage and the long even sway of the wheels pull her toward sleep before she has read the rest of what some stranger meant so badly to say. The paper is soft under her fingers, the ink a little blurred already where her thumb has rested too long against it.

choices.label: Hold the thread and go on.

nodes.body: However far Vera has taken the letter, read whole or only half glimpsed, what settles in her afterward is the same small, specific wrongness: a promise written down by someone who meant it entirely. No way now to know if it was ever kept. It is not an answer, only a splinter of one. It will not be reasoned back out of her, the way one true thing rarely can be once it has lodged. She finds herself wondering, uselessly, whether the letter's writer ever finished this exact stretch of track, or whether the promise itself was the whole of what they carried north. The train takes a long curve and the letter shifts in her lap as though it, too, were listening for what comes next. Vera feels the whole journey draw in tight around that unfinished promise, like breath held a beat too long. Something in her, quietly, decides to keep going.

She folds the letter back along its old creases, more carefully than whoever left it there. She carries the weight of that unkept promise with her down the length of the train, through cold vestibules and swaying couplings, past a dining car lit by a single working lamp. Somewhere ahead a child's unhurried voice threads under the noise of the wheels, and further on she can just make out low, murmuring conversation from passengers who do not seem, on the whole, to be talking about anything so simple as their destination. None of it explains outright who the letter was meant for, or whether its writer ever finished the journey it describes. Every detail seems to lean the same direction, north and further north, as though the whole train has been quietly built around this one unanswered promise. Vera steadies herself against the cold rail and goes to find out what the night intends to ask of her next.

The carriage is warm, warmer than the letter's cold ink deserves, and Vera's eyes close before she has decided to let them. She means only to rest a moment with the page still in her lap. The moment lengthens, and by the time she surfaces the light through the window has turned the flat grey of an ordinary morning. The letter is still folded in her hand, but when she opens it again the ink has smeared past reading, blurred by damp or by handling or simply by however long she slept holding it too tightly. She cannot now say what the promise was, only that there had been one, and that she came very close to knowing whom it was made to before the page gave the words up entirely. She arrives safe, unharmed, and empty-handed in the one way that matters.

Vera turns instead to the window, to the black country sliding past it mile after mile. The whole carriage sways with her as she leans her forehead close to the cold glass. Her reflection rides there too, pale and half-transparent over the dark, so that for a moment she cannot tell whether she is watching the land or watching herself fail to see it clearly. At first there is nothing to make out beyond her own doubled face, only the vague suggestion of fields going by, fences, the black shapes of trees. But as her eyes adjust she begins to notice that the land itself does not look like land she recognizes anymore, though she cannot yet say what exactly has changed about it. She presses her palm flat against the glass, half expecting the cold to sharpen her eyes the way it sharpens everything else, and finds instead that the harder she looks, the less certain the shapes outside become. Somewhere out there the flat country of home has become something else, something with edges she has not agreed to.

There are two ways to watch a landscape that has quietly stopped being the one you started in. Vera can feel herself torn between them. She could press her whole attention to the glass, study the changing shapes outside as directly and hungrily as she can manage, and risk whatever it is that notices being watched back. That is the bolder reading, the one that wants an answer now. Or she could let her gaze soften, take the country in sideways the way one takes in a shy animal, trusting that it will show more of itself to someone who does not seem to be looking too hard. Watching directly might tell her plainly what the land has become and why, at the cost of drawing whatever attention a too-eager stare tends to draw. Watching patiently costs her only the slow accumulation of small, uncertain details, and the chance that the night will keep its full shape hidden a while longer.

Vera presses close to the glass and studies the country outside without blinking. The fact she comes away with is small, exact, and impossible to unsee: the fences along the line have stopped, entirely, replaced somewhere back along the miles by an unbroken white that does not look like snow so much as absence, a country with nothing left in it to fence. There is no gradual transition she can point to, only the fact of it, arrived at all at once the way a held note becomes silence. She scans back along the glass as though the missing fences might still be caught somewhere at the edge of her vision, and finds only the same clean, fenceless white stretching on in every direction she tries. It will not sit still in her, this vanished boundary between one field and the next. It points her onward, deeper into the train, toward whoever might be able to say when exactly the fences stopped.

Vera softens her gaze instead and lets the country come to her sideways. What she notices this way is quieter but no less strange: the shapes outside are not passing at the speed a train should be passing them, slower in places, faster in others, as though the night itself were breathing unevenly. The unevenness has a rhythm to it, almost, if she does not try too hard to count it, the way a tide has a rhythm even when no single wave repeats the last exactly. She could carry that observation forward, weigh it against everything else the carriage has shown her, and let it sharpen into something she can use. Or she could let the rhythm of the glass and the low drone of the wheels lull her the rest of the way under, the way staring too long at moving water eventually pulls a tired mind down with it. The window is cold against her forehead, and sleep, when it comes for her here, comes gently, almost kindly.

choices.label: Hold the thread and go on.

nodes.body: However Vera has come to notice it, staring hard at the glass or letting the country arrive sideways, what she knows now settles into the same small, unshakeable wrongness. The land outside no longer keeps the rules land is supposed to keep, not its fences, not its own steady speed past the window. It is not a fact she can explain to anyone, and certainly not one that will explain itself back into comfort, but it will not loosen its hold on her either, the way a strange word overheard in an otherwise ordinary sentence keeps returning long after the sentence itself has been forgotten. The train takes a long curve and the whole black country outside seems, for a moment, to hold itself as still as she does, the way any wide thing holds itself before a decision. Vera lets the breath go, and understands that she means to keep watching.

She pulls back from the glass at last, carrying the fact of the fenceless, uneven country with her down the swaying length of the train, through vestibules where the cold gets in around the door frames and couplings that groan with every curve. Somewhere ahead, dishes clink faintly in an unlit dining car, and further on a child's voice threads calm and untroubled beneath the noise of the wheels, as if none of this were strange to her at all. She glances back once at the corridor's last window as she goes. The country there still refuses to settle into anything she can name, sliding by at its own uneven pace regardless of who is or is not watching it. None of it explains outright what has become of the world past the window. Every sound and every detail seems to lean the same way, further north, further into whatever the night has been quietly building toward since the platform clock stopped keeping time. Vera steadies herself against the cold rail and goes to meet it.

The glass is cold and the carriage is warm. Somewhere between the two Vera's eyes close before she means to let them. She surfaces later into the flat grey light of an ordinary morning. The window beside her shows only ordinary country now, fenced fields, black trees, nothing that argues with what a landscape should be. She cannot say when the fences came back, or whether they were ever truly gone, only that she was watching for something and then, without deciding to, she was not watching at all. She arrives unharmed, entirely safe, and troubled by nothing she can point to, only by the small, persistent sense that the night had been showing her something true, and that she let the snow and the warmth of the carriage lull her past the moment it mattered.

Vera makes her way to the dining car, drawn by the four regulars seated at the corner table. The carriage sways under her as the couplings take up the slack between cars. The long window at the end of the car throws her reflection back over the black rush of the night, doubled and faint, so that for a moment four seated shadows and one standing one all seem to share the glass together. The four at the table have menus open in front of them, have had them open, she guesses, for some time, and not one of the four has lifted a hand to call the steward or lift a fork. Their plates are clean, their glasses empty. Yet none of them looks impatient, or hungry, or even particularly awake. It is the stillness of the scene that catches her first, the way four people can sit so long at an empty table without once disturbing it.

There are two ways to read four regulars who never order. Vera turns both over before she moves. She could walk straight to their table and ask outright why they keep their seats through a meal they never touch, risking whatever it costs to interrupt people who seem, by every sign, content to be left exactly as they are. Or she could take an empty table nearby and watch a while first, note who speaks to whom and how the steward treats them, and let the pattern declare itself before she asks anything of it. Pressing in directly might get her a plain answer, or a closed door and four faces suddenly aware of being looked at. Waiting costs her nothing but time, and the risk that whatever keeps this table so still will simply go on being still, unexplained, for as long as she is willing to watch it. The four of them do not so much as glance her way while she decides, which somehow makes the choice feel larger rather than smaller.

Vera walks up and asks them plainly why they never order. The four regulars answer her together, in the mild, unbothered tone of people repeating something asked of them often. They are not hungry, one says, and have not been for longer than she would believe. They keep the table because the table is theirs, has always been theirs, on this train and on every one like it that has ever run this line. None of the four elaborates further. None seems to feel the need to, as though the answer were complete the moment it left their mouths. It is a small fact, oddly delivered, and it will not sit still in her afterward, this idea of passengers who ride without eating and without end. It points her further down the train, toward whatever else rides this line the same patient, permanent way.

Vera takes the table across the aisle instead and watches. The pattern she notices is this: the steward passes their table without pausing, without so much as a glance, as though it were as ordinary to him as an empty seat. No one at the corner table minds the omission. No one asks for anything to be brought. The four of them exchange a word now and then, low and unhurried, in the manner of people who have long since run out of anything urgent to say to one another and have made a kind of peace with that. She could carry that small, quiet detail forward and let it join everything else she has begun to notice about the night. Or she could let her own tiredness answer for her, let the warm dining car and the hum of the wheels carry her toward sleep before she has decided what the steward's indifference actually means. The lamp above her table gutters low and gold. It would be so easy, just now, to close her eyes.

choices.label: Hold the thread and go on.

nodes.body: However Vera has learned it, by asking outright or by watching the steward's practiced indifference, what settles in her is the same small wrongness: four passengers who neither eat nor leave, kept apart in a way the whole car seems to have quietly agreed to. It is not an explanation, only a fact with edges. It will not be reasoned away, the way one strange guest at an otherwise ordinary table changes how the whole room reads. She finds herself wondering how long the four of them have been riding this exact line together, and whether they boarded as strangers or as something closer than that. The train rocks through a long curve and the four regulars sway together in their seats, unbothered, permanent. Vera feels the journey close in around the thought. Something in her, carefully, decides to keep asking.

She leaves the dining car carrying the fact of the four regulars with her, out through the cold vestibule and into the next carriage, where a small girl sits wide awake at the far end, humming something low under the noise of the wheels. Further on, she can just make out a man's voice counting under his breath, and past him, seated alone, a woman dressed entirely in grey. Each of them, she notices now, keeps to the same kind of patient stillness the four regulars kept, as though the whole train had agreed on one unspoken manner of waiting. None of what she has learned tonight, not the clock, not the berth, not the letter, not the fenceless country, explains outright why this train carries passengers who never eat and never quite arrive. Every detail leans the same way, toward some larger truth about who is riding here and why. Vera steadies herself against the cold handrail and goes to find out.

Vera does not get the whole of it, not tonight. She keeps something true: four faces that have sat that table on every run of this line, never hungry, never leaving, watched over by a steward who has long since stopped finding them strange. It does not tell her who they are or what they are waiting for, only that they are waiting, patiently, permanently, the way certain things wait once they have stopped needing an answer. She wonders, walking away, whether she will still remember their faces clearly by morning, or whether they will blur the way dreams blur, true in feeling long after the details have gone soft. She carries the thought back to her own seat and holds it there, turning it over. It does not frighten her so much as it settles into her the way true things do, quietly, and for good. It is not the whole picture, but it is real, and it is hers now to keep.

Vera goes toward the small child sitting wide awake at the end of the carriage. The train sways beneath her with each length of rail, a motion the girl does not seem to notice at all. In the window past the child's shoulder, Vera's own reflection rides along the dark, pale and doubled, watching itself approach. The girl cannot be more than seven or eight, no coat over her nightdress, no adult anywhere near her. Yet she sits with her hands folded, perfectly calm, humming some tune too old and too slow for a child that age to know. Her feet, too small to reach the floor, swing gently in time with the melody rather than with the rocking of the carriage, as though she were keeping her own private rhythm entirely apart from the train's. It is the calm that catches Vera first, more than the humming, more than the hour: a child alone on a train at this hour ought to be frightened, or at least tired. This one is neither.

There are two ways to approach a child this calm at this hour. Vera feels both pulling at her. She could sit down directly and ask her plainly where her parents are, and risk whatever a straightforward question does to a girl who may simply prefer not to be asked. Or she could linger nearby first, let the child's own unhurried humming tell her something before she interrupts it, and trust that patience will earn more than a question fired too quickly. Asking outright might get her a name, a destination, an answer to hold onto, at the cost of breaking whatever quiet the girl has built around herself. Waiting costs her only the discomfort of not knowing yet, and the chance that the girl's calm, once disturbed, does not come back so easily. The girl, for her part, seems entirely unbothered by being watched, as if she has been waited on by careful adults before and found the waiting harmless.

Vera sits and asks her directly. The girl answers without any of the wariness a stranger's question usually earns from a child: she is riding to see her grandmother, she says, the way she does every year at this time, though when Vera asks which stop that is, the girl only shrugs and says it is the one that comes when it is ready. She says this the way another child might say the number of a favorite platform, with no sense at all that the answer is strange. It is a small, plain fact, delivered without a flicker of doubt. It will not sit still afterward: a stop that arrives on its own schedule, known to a child and to no timetable Vera has seen. It points her further into the train, toward whatever else on this line keeps its own private hours.

Vera sits a little way off and listens instead. What she notices is this: the girl's humming never once breaks its pattern, the same handful of notes circling back on themselves like a wheel that has forgotten how to stop turning. No one comes to check on her. She does not look for anyone to come, her attention fixed instead on some point past the window that Vera cannot quite locate herself. The melody, the longer Vera listens to it, begins to feel less like a lullaby and more like a small, patient argument the girl is making to herself. Vera could carry that small observation forward and let it deepen alongside everything else the night has offered her. Or she could let the same slow circling rhythm work on her the way it seems to have worked on the girl, easing her, without her quite noticing, toward sleep. The car is warm and dim. The tune, once it has caught in her ear, is difficult to set down again.

choices.label: Hold the thread and go on.

nodes.body: Whichever way Vera has come to it, by a direct question answered too easily or by a tune overheard too long, what she now knows about the child settles into the same small wrongness: a girl who rides this line as a matter of course, toward a stop that keeps its own hours and answers to no timetable. It is not enough to explain the whole of the night. It will not be talked back out of her either, the way a nursery rhyme half remembered keeps circling long after the room has gone quiet. She finds herself wondering how many years the girl has already made this exact same journey, and whether a grandmother waiting at a stop with no fixed hour ever grows impatient. The train sways through another curve and the girl's humming rises and falls with it, patient, untroubled. Vera feels the journey draw close around the thought, and understands that she means to follow it further.

She leaves the child humming at the end of the carriage and carries the thought of that private, self-scheduling stop with her back through the train, past the dining car where four regulars keep their table in permanent, wordless patience. Further on, a man's voice counts something under his breath, station names, she thinks, though none of them ring familiar, and beyond him a woman in grey sits alone with a grief she has not spoken aloud to anyone. Each of them, in their own way, seems to be waiting on a schedule as private and untroubled as the girl's own. None of it explains outright why a child would ride toward a stop that arrives when it is ready. Every detail leans the same way, further into the question of who these passengers really are. Vera steadies herself against the cold rail and goes on to find out.

Vera does not learn everything the child could tell her, not tonight. She keeps one true thing: a girl who rides this line without fear because she has ridden it before, toward a stop that keeps its own private hours and has never yet failed to arrive when it is ready. It does not complete the larger picture, does not tell her what waits at that stop or why a child should travel it alone. It settles into her the way a small, certain fact settles, whole and undeniable. She thinks, walking away, of how easily the girl trusted a stop she could not name to arrive exactly when it needed to, and wonders what it would take to trust anything that completely herself. She carries it back to her own seat, turning it slowly, and finds that what she has learned unsettles her less than it should, and stays with her all the same. It is hers now, real, and worth the keeping.

Vera follows the low, steady murmur to a man seated by himself near the end of the carriage, counting something under his breath in time with the wheels. The carriage sways with the rail joints, and in the dark window opposite her own reflection keeps pace, watching her watch him. He has a small, worn notebook open on his knee, and as she draws closer she can make out what he is saying: station names, one after another, ticked off in a murmur that never quite rises to a full voice. None of the names match anything on the printed timetable she glimpsed earlier. Yet he says them with the flat, practiced certainty of a man reciting a route he has traveled a thousand times. It is the certainty that catches her, more than the strangeness of the names themselves.

There are two ways to approach a man counting stations that never come. Vera weighs them both. She could sit across from him and ask outright what list he is keeping and why, and risk whatever a direct question does to a man clearly absorbed in something private and precise. Or she could linger near enough to catch the names without interrupting the count, and trust that a list recited often enough will eventually explain itself to a patient listener. Asking plainly might get her the whole of his reasoning at once, at the cost of breaking a rhythm he may not easily find again. Waiting costs her only the slow accumulation of names she cannot yet place, and the risk that his count will simply go on, unexplained, mile after uncounted mile. He has not looked up once since she noticed him, which makes either choice feel, oddly, like an intrusion on something private.

Vera asks him directly what he is counting. He looks up as if surprised anyone would need to ask, then tells her plainly: he is counting the stations this line was meant to serve before they were taken off the schedule, one by one, over years he does not name, though the train still passes each abandoned platform whether or not anyone remembers to expect it. It is a small, hard fact, delivered without any doubt at all. It will not sit still afterward, this idea of stations still standing, still passed, though no one stops there and no one is meant to remember them. It points her further into the train, toward whoever else might know how many stops a line can lose before it stops being the line it was.

Vera listens instead from a little distance. What she gathers this way is a rhythm more than a fact: his count never falters, never repeats a name twice, and never once seems to reach an end he is satisfied with. Each name, when she catches it clearly, sounds ordinary enough on its own, some small town or crossing or halt. It is only the sheer unbroken length of the recitation that begins to feel wrong the longer she listens to it. She could carry that steady, unfinished rhythm forward and let it settle alongside everything else the carriage has shown her. Or she could let its very steadiness work the same way the wheels do, lulling her by increments toward sleep before she has understood what he is truly keeping track of. The car is warm, the murmur almost musical. It would take so little, just now, to let her eyes close on the sound of it.

nodes.body: Whichever way Vera has come to it, by asking outright or by listening from the edge of his private ritual, what settles in her is the same small wrongness: a line with more stations behind it than ahead, stations dropped and yet somehow still passed, remembered by one man with a notebook and by no one else. It is not enough to explain the whole shape of the night. It will not loosen its grip either, the way a name once learned refuses to go back to being unknown. She pictures, briefly and without meaning to, all those darkened platforms sliding past the windows unremarked, waiting on a memory kept by exactly one tired passenger. The train sways through another long curve and his count continues underneath it, patient, unbroken. Vera feels the journey close around the thought, and understands, quietly, that she is not done following it.

She leaves him to his count and carries the thought of the vanished stations back through the swaying train, past the dining car where four passengers keep an untouched table, past the child humming her own patient tune at the end of another carriage. Somewhere ahead a woman dressed in grey sits with a grief that has not yet found its way into words. Vera senses, without being able to prove it, that whatever this man has been counting connects to her too, and to the child, and to the four at the table who never eat. Each of them, in their own separate way, seems to be keeping some private ledger the rest of the train has agreed not to ask about. None of it resolves outright. Every detail leans the same direction, toward some larger accounting the whole train seems quietly engaged in. She steadies herself against the cold rail and carries on to find it.

Vera does not learn the whole of his count, not tonight. She keeps one true thing: a line with more stations lost than kept, still passed in the dark whether or not anyone remembers to look for them, tallied faithfully by a man who may be the last one keeping the tally at all. It does not tell her why the stations vanished, or what waits at whichever one comes next. It settles into her the way an old, sad fact settles, complete in itself even without a larger frame around it. She thinks of him still murmuring away in the dark carriage behind her, keeping his lonely and exact accounting for an audience of no one, and finds the thought oddly companionable rather than sad. She carries it back to her seat, turning the number over. It does not undo her, only weighs a little, honestly, in her chest. It is real, and she means to keep it.

Vera makes her way toward the woman seated alone in grey, travelling, by every outward sign, in mourning. The carriage sways beneath her with the same patient rhythm it has kept all night. Her own reflection rides the dark window behind the woman's shoulder, pale and doubled, watching the meeting before it happens. The woman sits with her hands folded in her lap exactly as the child's were folded three carriages back, her coat and dress and even the scarf at her throat all the same unbroken grey. She has not moved, as far as Vera can tell, since the train left the platform. Her gaze rests somewhere just past the window, fixed on nothing Vera can identify, the way a person looks when the thing they are actually seeing is not outside the glass at all. It is the stillness that catches her first, a grief held so carefully in place that it looks, from a distance, almost like peace.

There are two ways to approach a grief held this still. Vera can feel herself weighing both at once. She could sit beside the woman directly and ask, gently but plainly, who she has lost and where she is travelling to see them laid to rest, and risk whatever a stranger's direct sympathy does to someone guarding her sorrow so carefully. Or she could take the seat across the aisle instead, offer nothing but her presence, and let the woman's own quiet, if she chooses to break it, come in its own time. Asking outright might bring the whole of her story forward at once, at the cost of trespassing on a grief that has clearly built its own walls. Waiting costs Vera only patience, and the chance that the woman's silence simply continues, undisturbed and unexplained, for as long as the night allows. Neither choice feels entirely right, which is itself, Vera suspects, exactly the kind of choice grief usually offers a stranger.

Vera asks her gently. The woman answers without flinching, in the flat, careful voice of someone who has said the words before and made her peace with saying them again: she buried her husband three winters past, she says, and has ridden this same train north every year since on the night closest to the date, though she could not say, if pressed, what exactly she expects to find at the end of it. It is a small, plain fact. It will not sit still in Vera afterward, this idea of a grief that repeats itself yearly on a train that may not exist for anyone else to ride. It points her further into the carriage, toward whoever else might understand what this line is truly for.

Vera sits across the aisle instead and watches. What she gathers without a single word exchanged is this: the woman's stillness is not emptiness but discipline, the careful holding of something that would otherwise break loose entirely. Once, and only once, the woman's folded hands tighten briefly against each other before easing again, the smallest possible sign of the effort the stillness is costing her. Vera could carry that quiet understanding forward, let it join the rest of what the night has shown her about who rides this train and why. Or she could let the woman's very stillness settle over her too, the way grief in a room can become a kind of hush that pulls everyone in it toward silence, and toward sleep. The carriage is warm, the woman's calm strangely contagious. It would be easy, here, to let her own eyes close before she has understood what she has seen.

nodes.body: Whichever way Vera has come to it, by a gentle question answered honestly or by a shared silence that told her almost as much, what she now understands about the woman in grey settles into the same small wrongness: a mourning repeated yearly on a train that keeps no ordinary schedule, ridden by someone who no longer quite expects an answer and rides anyway. It is not enough to explain the whole of the night. It will not be reasoned back out of her, the way another person's grief, once witnessed honestly, stays lodged where it lands. She finds herself wondering what it would take to keep returning to the same unanswered place year after year, and whether she herself has ever loved anything that faithfully. The train sways through a long curve and the woman's folded hands do not move. Vera feels the journey close around the thought, and understands that she is not yet finished with it.

She leaves the woman to her stillness and carries the thought of that yearly, unfinished mourning back through the train, past the four regulars keeping their silent table, past the child humming her patient tune. Somewhere ahead a man murmurs the names of stations that no longer exist. Vera senses, without proof, that his list and her grief and the child's private schedule are all threads of the same larger cloth, though she cannot yet see the whole of the weave. She finds herself walking a little more carefully than before, as though the carriage itself has grown heavier with everything she now knows it is carrying. None of it resolves outright. Every detail leans the same way, deeper into the question of what kind of passengers this train has agreed to carry. She steadies herself against the cold rail and goes on to learn more.

Vera does not learn everything the woman carries, not tonight. She keeps one true thing: a grief so carefully tended that it has become its own kind of ritual, repeated yearly on a train that seems to exist, at least in part, to hold it. It does not tell her what the woman hopes to find at the end of the line, or whether she has ever found it. It settles into Vera whole and unmistakable, the way another person's sorrow does when it is witnessed rather than explained away. She thinks of the woman's folded hands, of the one small tremor she allowed herself, and understands that some griefs are not meant to be solved, only accompanied for however long the accompanying is offered. She carries it back to her own seat and sits with it a while. It does not undo her, only asks something quiet of her in return. It is real. It is hers to carry now too.

The conductor finds Vera again near the vestibule where the rail begins its long climb into higher ground. The carriage sways harder now, as though the grade itself were arguing with the wheels. In the black glass beside them both, her reflection and his stand together over the dark, doubled and strangely companionable, two shapes riding the same uncertain light. He has his silver punch-clock out again, snapping it open and shut in the same restless rhythm she noticed hours ago. This time he pauses, catches her eye, and does not immediately look away, as though some part of him has been waiting for her to come find him a second time. The lamp overhead gutters with the strain of the climb, and for a moment his whole face is lit gold and uncertain, a man weighing something he has clearly weighed before. It is that pause, more than anything he has done all night, that tells her he might, if pressed properly now, actually answer.

There are two ways to press the conductor now that he has paused. Vera feels the weight of both. She could ask him outright, here in the cold vestibule with the grade steepening under them, exactly what waits at the tunnel ahead and why he keeps consulting a clock that answers to no ordinary hour. Or she could hold her question a moment longer, let his own hesitation finish its work, and trust that a man who has already paused once will finish the thought himself if she gives him room to. Asking directly might get her the plain truth before the tunnel arrives, at the cost of pushing a cautious man back into his old silence. Waiting risks nothing but the shortening distance to the tunnel mouth, and the chance that his nerve, once broken, does not mend itself before the dark closes over them both. The mountain ahead offers her no further time to weigh it than that.

Vera asks him plainly what waits beyond the tunnel. This time the conductor answers, quietly, as though relieved finally to be asked directly. The tunnel, he says, is not a passage through the mountain so much as a passage through the night itself. What a passenger carries into it is generally what that passenger carries back out, changed or unchanged depending on how honestly they entered. He says it the way a man says something he has watched prove true more times than he cares to count, without embellishment and without doubt. It is a strange, exact answer for so plain a question. It will not sit still in her afterward: a tunnel that asks something of its travellers rather than simply delaying them. It points her onward, toward whatever choice waits at the mouth of it.

Vera lets her question wait a moment longer, and watches instead as the conductor's own hesitation plays itself out: he opens the punch-clock, studies it, closes it again, and murmurs something about the tunnel under his breath that she does not quite catch, though the tone of it is closer to warning than to simple timekeeping. His hand does not quite steady itself around the silver case this time, a small tremor she has not noticed in him before tonight. She could carry that overheard fragment forward, weigh it against everything the night has already shown her about this line and what it asks of its passengers. Or she could let the climbing grade and the low, steady effort of the engine lull her instead, the way the whole night has been trying, gently and insistently, to lull her. The vestibule is cold. The carriage behind her is warm, and warmth, just now, argues its own case.
